files_to_create.description: Recursive descent parser with Pratt expression parsing. Contains Parser struct, all parse_* functions, precedence table, and error recovery logic.
Parse error types using thiserror + miette. Rich diagnostics with source spans, expected/found tokens, and recovery information.

files_to_modify.description: Add `pub mod parser;` and `pub mod error;` declarations. Re-export the public parse API: `parse()` function and `ParseResult` type.
No changes needed â€” axiom-lexer, thiserror, and miette are already declared as dependencies. insta is already a dev-dependency.
Wire `--emit=ast` to call `axiom_parser::parse()` and pretty-print the result.
changes: In the `Some("ast")` match arm: lex source, call `axiom_parser::parse()`, print AST debug output
Print parse errors to stderr using miette formatting

axiom_parser::parse.description: Main entry point. Lexes and parses an AXIOM source string into a Module AST. Returns all parse errors collected during recovery.

axiom_parser::parser::Parser.description: The recursive descent parser. Consumes a token stream and produces AST nodes. Not exposed directly â€” users call `parse()` instead.

methods.signature: fn new(source: &'src str, tokens: Vec<Token>) -> Self
methods.description: Create parser from source text and pre-lexed token stream.
Parse a complete module. Entry point for the recursive descent.

axiom_parser::error::ParseError.signature: #[derive(Debug, Clone, thiserror::Error, miette::Diagnostic)] pub enum ParseError { ... }
axiom_parser::error::ParseError.description: All possible parse errors, each with span information for miette rendering.
variants: UnexpectedToken { expected: String, found: String, span: SourceSpan }
InvalidAnnotation { name: String, reason: String, span: SourceSpan }
MissingClosingDelimiter { delimiter: char, open_span: SourceSpan, span: SourceSpan }

axiom_parser::error::ParseResult.signature: pub struct ParseResult { pub module: Module, pub errors: Vec<ParseError> }
axiom_parser::error::ParseResult.description: Result of parsing. Always returns a Module (possibly partial due to error recovery) and all collected errors. A parse is considered successful when errors is empty.

technical_spec:
# M1.2 Parser Technical Specification

## 1. Architecture Overview

The parser is a hand-written recursive descent parser with Pratt parsing for expressions. It consumes a `Vec<Token>` produced by `axiom_lexer::Lexer` and produces a typed `Module` AST defined in `ast.rs`.

### Design Principles
- **Error recovery**: On parse error, skip to next synchronization point and continue parsing. Collect ALL errors.
- **Span tracking**: Every AST node is wrapped in `Spanned<T>` with byte-offset spans from the original source.
- **No panics**: The parser never panics. All error paths produce `ParseError` values.
- **Zero allocations where possible**: Reuse the source string; identifiers are cloned from the token's string data.

## 2. Parser State

```rust
pub(crate) struct Parser<'src> {
    source: &'src str,
    tokens: Vec<Token>,
    pos: usize,           // current position in token stream
    errors: Vec<ParseError>,
}
```

### Token Navigation Methods

```rust
impl<'src> Parser<'src> {
    /// Peek at current token without consuming.
    fn peek(&self) -> &TokenKind;

    /// Peek at the token `n` positions ahead.
    fn peek_nth(&self, n: usize) -> &TokenKind;

    /// Get the current token's span.
    fn current_span(&self) -> Span;

    /// Advance to next token, returning the consumed token.
    fn advance(&mut self) -> &Token;

    /// Consume current token if it matches expected kind. Returns true on match.
    fn eat(&mut self, kind: &TokenKind) -> bool;

    /// Consume current token if it matches expected kind, else push error and return None.
    fn expect(&mut self, kind: &TokenKind, expected_desc: &str) -> Option<Span>;

    /// Check if current token matches without consuming.
    fn check(&self, kind: &TokenKind) -> bool;

    /// Check if current token is an identifier with a specific name.
    fn check_ident(&self, name: &str) -> bool;

    /// Check if we've reached EOF.
    fn at_end(&self) -> bool;
}
```

Note on `check` / `eat` / `expect` for tokens with payloads (like `Ident(String)`): These methods use `std::mem::discriminant` comparison for variants with data. A separate `eat_ident()` / `expect_ident()` is used when we need to match AND extract the identifier string.

## 3. Grammar Productions

Each grammar production maps to a `parse_*` method. Below is the complete set.

### 3.1 Module (Top-Level)

```
Module ::= ModuleAnnotations Item*
ModuleAnnotations ::= (Annotation ';'?)*   // module-level: @module, @intent, @constraint, @target
```

```rust
fn parse_module(&mut self) -> Module
```

Logic:
1. Collect leading annotations (before any `fn`/`struct`/`type`/`import`).
2. If an `@module` annotation is found, extract its argument as the module name.
3. Parse items in a loop until EOF.
4. On error, synchronize to the next item-start token (`fn`, `struct`, `type`, `import`, `@`, EOF).

### 3.2 Items

```
Item ::= Function | StructDef | TypeAlias | ImportDecl
```

```rust
fn parse_item(&mut self) -> Option<Spanned<Item>>
```

Logic:
1. Collect any annotations preceding the item.
2. Match on the current token:
   - `Fn` -> `parse_function(annotations)`
   - `Struct` -> `parse_struct(annotations)`
   - `Type` -> `parse_type_alias(annotations)`
   - `Import` -> `parse_import(annotations)`
   - Otherwise -> error, synchronize to next item start.

### 3.3 Function Definition

```
Function ::= Annotation* 'fn' IDENT '(' ParamList? ')' '->' TypeExpr Block
ParamList ::= Param (',' Param)* ','?
Param ::= IDENT ':' TypeExpr Annotation*
```

```rust
fn parse_function(&mut self, annotations: Vec<Spanned<Annotation>>) -> Spanned<Item>
fn parse_param_list(&mut self) -> Vec<Param>
fn parse_param(&mut self) -> Option<Param>
```

Notes:
- Parameters may carry inline annotations like `@layout(row_major)` and `@align(64)`. These appear AFTER the type expression.
- Return type annotation also supports inline annotations: `-> tensor[f32, M, N] @layout(row_major)`.
- Trailing comma in parameter list is allowed.

### 3.4 Struct Definition

```
StructDef ::= Annotation* 'struct' IDENT '{' StructField (',' StructField)* ','? '}'
StructField ::= IDENT ':' TypeExpr Annotation*
```

```rust
fn parse_struct(&mut self, annotations: Vec<Spanned<Annotation>>) -> Spanned<Item>
```

### 3.5 Type Alias

```
TypeAlias ::= 'type' IDENT '=' TypeExpr ';'
```

```rust
fn parse_type_alias(&mut self, annotations: Vec<Spanned<Annotation>>) -> Spanned<Item>
```

### 3.6 Import

```
Import ::= 'import' IDENT ('::' IDENT)* ('as' IDENT)? ';'
```

Note: `as` is not a keyword token. We check for the identifier "as".

```rust
fn parse_import(&mut self, annotations: Vec<Spanned<Annotation>>) -> Spanned<Item>
```

### 3.7 Annotations

```
Annotation ::= '@' IDENT
             | '@' IDENT '(' AnnotationArgList ')'
             | '@' IDENT '{' AnnotationKVList '}'
             | '@' IDENT IDENT+          // e.g., @complexity O(n^3) â€” special case
```

```rust
fn parse_annotation(&mut self) -> Option<Spanned<Annotation>>
fn parse_annotation_args(&mut self) -> Vec<AnnotationValue>
fn parse_annotation_kv_list(&mut self) -> Vec<(String, AnnotationValue)>
fn parse_annotation_value(&mut self) -> Option<AnnotationValue>
```

Annotation dispatch (based on the `@name` string from the `TokenKind::Annotation(name)`):

| Annotation Name   | Form                          | AST Variant                     |
|-------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| `module`          | `@module name;`               | `Annotation::Module(String)`     |
| `pure`            | `@pure`                       | `Annotation::Pure`               |
| `const`           | `@const`                      | `Annotation::Const`              |
| `inline`          | `@inline(always|never|hint)`  | `Annotation::Inline(InlineHint)` |
| `complexity`      | `@complexity O(n^3)`          | `Annotation::Complexity(String)` |
| `intent`          | `@intent("description")`      | `Annotation::Intent(String)`     |
| `constraint`      | `@constraint { k: v, ... }`   | `Annotation::Constraint(Vec<...>)` |
| `target`          | `@target { ... }`             | `Annotation::Target(Vec<String>)` |
| `strategy`        | `@strategy { ... }`           | `Annotation::Strategy(StrategyBlock)` |
| `vectorizable`    | `@vectorizable(dims)`         | `Annotation::Vectorizable(Vec<String>)` |
| `parallel`        | `@parallel(dims)`             | `Annotation::Parallel(Vec<String>)` |
| `layout`          | `@layout(kind)`               | `Annotation::Layout(LayoutKind)` |
| `align`           | `@align(bytes)`               | `Annotation::Align(u64)`        |
| other             | `@name(args)` or `@name{kv}`  | `Annotation::Custom(name, args)` |

Special parsing for `@complexity`: The tokens after `@complexity` until the next newline-delimited statement or known delimiter are collected as a raw string (e.g., `O(n^3)`). Since the lexer produces `Ident("O")`, `LParen`, `Ident("n")`, `Caret`, `IntLiteral(3)`, `RParen` for `O(n^3)`, we reconstruct the string from spans.

Special parsing for `@target { cpu.simd }`: The braces contain dot-separated identifiers and commas. `cpu.simd` lexes as `Ident("cpu")`, `Dot`, `Ident("simd")`. We collect these as dot-joined strings.

Special parsing for `@constraint { correctness: "IEEE 754 compliant" }`: Standard key-value parsing where keys are identifiers and values are `AnnotationValue`.

### 3.8 Strategy Block

```
StrategyBlock ::= '@strategy' '{' StrategyEntry* '}'
StrategyEntry ::= IDENT ':' StrategyValue
StrategyValue ::= '?' IDENT              // optimization hole
                | '{' (IDENT ':' StrategyValue ',')* '}'
                | AnnotationValue          // concrete value
```

```rust
fn parse_strategy_block(&mut self) -> StrategyBlock
fn parse_strategy_entry(&mut self) -> Option<(String, StrategyValue)>
fn parse_strategy_value(&mut self) -> StrategyValue
```

Notes:
- Strategy entries are NOT separated by commas or semicolons in the sample files. They are separated by newlines, but since our lexer ignores whitespace, entries are delimited by the pattern: `IDENT ':' Value`. We detect the start of a new entry by seeing an identifier followed by `:` (lookahead).
- Nested maps (like `tiling: { M: ?tile_m, N: ?tile_n, K: ?tile_k }`) use commas or whitespace-separation within inner braces.

### 3.9 Type Expressions

```
TypeExpr ::= NamedType | TensorType | ArrayType | SliceType | PtrType | TupleType | FnType
NamedType ::= IDENT                          // i32, MyStruct, M
TensorType ::= 'tensor' '[' TypeExpr (',' DimExpr)* ']'
ArrayType ::= 'array' '[' TypeExpr ',' Expr ']'
SliceType ::= 'slice' '[' TypeExpr ']'
PtrType ::= 'ptr' '[' TypeExpr ']'
TupleType ::= '(' TypeExpr (',' TypeExpr)+ ')'
FnType ::= 'fn' '(' TypeExprList? ')' '->' TypeExpr

DimExpr ::= INT_LITERAL | IDENT | '?'
```

```rust
fn parse_type_expr(&mut self) -> TypeExpr
fn parse_dim_expr(&mut self) -> DimExpr
```

Type keyword tokens (`I8`, `I16`, ..., `F64`, `Bool`) are mapped to `TypeExpr::Named("i32")` etc. The `Tensor`, `Array`, `Slice`, `Ptr` keyword tokens trigger their respective compound type parsing.

### 3.10 Statements

```
Stmt ::= LetStmt | ReturnStmt | IfStmt | ForStmt | WhileStmt | AssignOrExprStmt
LetStmt ::= 'let' IDENT ':' TypeExpr '=' Expr ';'
ReturnStmt ::= 'return' Expr ';'
IfStmt ::= 'if' Expr Block ('else' Block)?
ForStmt ::= 'for' IDENT ':' TypeExpr 'in' Expr Block
WhileStmt ::= 'while' Expr Block
AssignOrExprStmt ::= Expr ('=' Expr)? ';'
```

```rust
fn parse_stmt(&mut self) -> Option<Spanned<Stmt>>
fn parse_let_stmt(&mut self) -> Spanned<Stmt>
fn parse_return_stmt(&mut self) -> Spanned<Stmt>
fn parse_if_stmt(&mut self) -> Spanned<Stmt>
fn parse_for_stmt(&mut self) -> Spanned<Stmt>
fn parse_while_stmt(&mut self) -> Spanned<Stmt>
fn parse_assign_or_expr_stmt(&mut self) -> Spanned<Stmt>
```

Notes:
- `let` does NOT support `mut` keyword in the current AST (the `mutable` field exists but is always false for now). We can extend this later.
  UPDATE: Actually the `Mut` keyword token exists. So we parse `let mut x: T = v;` and set `mutable: true`.
- `if` does NOT require parentheses around the condition (unlike C). The condition is any expression parsed up to the `{`.
- `for` uses the `in` keyword, not `:` for the iterable.
- `AssignOrExprStmt`: Parse an expression. If followed by `=`, it's an assignment (`target = value;`). Otherwise it's an expression statement (`expr;`). The semicolon is consumed.

### 3.11 Block

```
Block ::= '{' Stmt* '}'
```

```rust
fn parse_block(&mut self) -> Block
```

Special: Strategy blocks (`@strategy { ... }`) can appear inside function bodies. When we see an `@strategy` annotation inside a block, we parse it and attach it as a statement-level annotation. Since the AST `Stmt` enum doesn't have an annotation variant, strategy blocks inside function bodies are parsed as `Stmt::Expr(Expr::StrategyBlock(...))` â€” BUT wait, the current `Expr` enum has no `StrategyBlock` variant. 

**Resolution**: Strategy blocks inside function bodies are parsed as standalone annotations attached to the Block. We add an `annotations` field to the `Block` struct:
```rust
pub struct Block {
    pub annotations: Vec<Spanned<Annotation>>,  // strategy blocks, etc.
    pub stmts: Vec<Spanned<Stmt>>,
}
```
Alternatively, we keep the existing Block struct and parse strategy blocks as part of the function's annotations (since `@strategy` appears in the function body before any loop code, it's semantically a function-level annotation). Looking at `matmul_naive.axm`, the `@strategy { ... }` block is INSIDE the function body, right after the opening brace. We will parse it as a standalone item inside the block and collect it into a `Vec<Spanned<Annotation>>` on the `Block`.

Actually, the simplest approach: when parsing statements inside a block, if we encounter an `Annotation` token, we parse the annotation and store it in the block's annotation list. The existing `Block` struct needs the `annotations` field added.

### 3.12 Expression Parsing (Pratt Parser)

The Pratt parser is the core of expression parsing. It handles precedence and associativity without explicit grammar rules for each level.

#### Binding Power Table

Pratt parsing uses "binding power" (bp) instead of precedence levels. Higher bp = tighter binding. Left-associative operators have `left_bp < right_bp`. Right-associative would have `left_bp > right_bp`.

| Precedence | Operators                             | Left BP | Right BP | Assoc |
|------------|---------------------------------------|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 (lowest) | `or`                                  | 1       | 2        | left  |
| 2          | `and`                                 | 3       | 4        | left  |
| 3          | `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`      | 5       | 6        | left  |
| 4          | `+`, `-`, `+%`, `+|`, `-%`, `-|`      | 7       | 8        | left  |
| 5          | `*`, `/`, `%`, `*%`                   | 9       | 10       | left  |
| 6 (prefix) | `-` (negate), `not`                   | â€”       | 11       | right |
| 7 (postfix)| `f(x)`, `a[i]`, `.field`, `.method()` | 13      | â€”        | left  |

#### Pratt Parser Functions

```rust
/// Parse an expression with the given minimum binding power.
fn parse_expr(&mut self) -> Expr {
    self.parse_expr_bp(0)
}

/// Core Pratt expression parser.
fn parse_expr_bp(&mut self, min_bp: u8) -> Expr {
    // 1. Parse prefix: literal, ident, unary op, grouped expr, etc.
    let mut lhs = self.parse_prefix();

    // 2. Loop: while next token is an infix/postfix op with bp >= min_bp
    loop {
        let (op, left_bp, right_bp) = match self.peek_infix_op() {
            Some(info) if info.left_bp >= min_bp => info,
            _ => break,
        };

        // Handle postfix operators (call, index, field access)
        if is_postfix(op) {
            lhs = self.parse_postfix(lhs);
            continue;
        }

        // Consume the operator token
        self.advance();

        // Recurse for the right-hand side
        let rhs = self.parse_expr_bp(right_bp);

        lhs = Expr::BinaryOp {
            op,
            lhs: Box::new(lhs),
            rhs: Box::new(rhs),
        };
    }

    lhs
}
```

#### Prefix Parsing

```rust
fn parse_prefix(&mut self) -> Expr
```

Handles:
- **Integer literal**: `IntLiteral { value, .. }` -> `Expr::IntLiteral(value)`
- **Float literal**: `FloatLiteral { value, .. }` -> `Expr::FloatLiteral(value)`
- **String literal**: `StringLiteral(s)` -> `Expr::StringLiteral(s)`
- **Bool literal**: `BoolLiteral(b)` -> `Expr::BoolLiteral(b)`
- **Identifier**: `Ident(s)` -> `Expr::Ident(s)`
- **Optimization hole**: `OptHole(s)` -> `Expr::OptHole(s)`
- **Unary minus**: `Minus` -> consume, parse operand with prefix bp (11), produce `Expr::UnaryOp { op: UnaryOp::Neg, operand }`
- **Unary not**: `Not` -> consume, parse operand with prefix bp (11), produce `Expr::UnaryOp { op: UnaryOp::Not, operand }`
- **Grouped expression**: `LParen` -> consume, parse expr, expect `RParen`, return inner expr. Also handles tuple expressions if there are commas (but tuples are not in the current Expr enum, so we only handle grouping for now).
- **Conversion keywords**: `Widen`/`Narrow`/`Truncate` -> parse as a call expression: consume keyword, expect `(`, parse arg, expect `)`. These become `Expr::Call { func: Expr::Ident("widen"), args: [arg] }`.

Special case â€” `tensor.zeros[f32, M, N]`: Lexes as `Ident("tensor")`, `Dot`, `Ident("zeros")`, `LBracket`, ... This is a method call with type parameters. We handle it as: parse `tensor` as ident, then `.zeros` as field access, then `[f32, M, N]` as index. So it becomes `Expr::Index { expr: Expr::FieldAccess { expr: Expr::Ident("tensor"), field: "zeros" }, indices: [...] }`. This is syntactically correct given our AST.

#### Infix Operator Lookup

```rust
/// Returns (BinOp, left_bp, right_bp) for the current token if it's an infix operator.
fn peek_infix_op(&self) -> Option<(InfixOp, u8, u8)>
```

Mapping from `TokenKind` to operator info:

| TokenKind    | BinOp          | left_bp | right_bp |
|-------------|----------------|---------|----------|
| `Or`        | `BinOp::Or`    | 1       | 2        |
| `And`       | `BinOp::And`   | 3       | 4        |
| `Eq`        | `BinOp::Eq`    | 5       | 6        |
| `NotEq`     | `BinOp::NotEq` | 5       | 6        |
| `Lt`        | `BinOp::Lt`    | 5       | 6        |
| `Gt`        | `BinOp::Gt`    | 5       | 6        |
| `LtEq`      | `BinOp::LtEq`  | 5       | 6        |
| `GtEq`      | `BinOp::GtEq`  | 5       | 6        |
| `Plus`      | `BinOp::Add`   | 7       | 8        |
| `Minus`     | `BinOp::Sub`   | 7       | 8        |
| `PlusWrap`  | `BinOp::AddWrap` | 7     | 8        |
| `PlusSat`   | `BinOp::AddSat`  | 7     | 8        |
| `MinusWrap` | `BinOp::SubWrap` | 7     | 8        |
| `MinusSat`  | `BinOp::SubSat`  | 7     | 8        |
| `Star`      | `BinOp::Mul`   | 9       | 10       |
| `Slash`     | `BinOp::Div`   | 9       | 10       |
| `Percent`   | `BinOp::Mod`   | 9       | 10       |
| `StarWrap`  | `BinOp::MulWrap` | 9     | 10       |

#### Postfix Parsing

Postfix operators have left_bp = 13 (highest). They are detected by peeking at the current token AFTER parsing the left-hand side.

```rust
fn parse_postfix(&mut self, lhs: Expr) -> Expr
```

Handles:
- **Call**: `LParen` -> parse argument list `(expr, expr, ...)`, produce `Expr::Call { func: lhs, args }`
- **Index**: `LBracket` -> parse index list `[expr, expr, ...]`, produce `Expr::Index { expr: lhs, indices }`
- **Field access**: `Dot` `Ident(field)` -> produce `Expr::FieldAccess { expr: lhs, field }`
- **Method call**: `Dot` `Ident(method)` `LParen` -> parse args, produce `Expr::MethodCall { expr: lhs, method, args }`

For Dot: peek ahead after consuming Dot. If the next-next token is `LParen`, it's a method call. Otherwise it's a field access. But we also need to handle `tensor.zeros[...]` which is field + index, not method.

Actual logic:
1. Consume `Dot`
2. Expect `Ident(name)`
3. If next token is `LParen` -> method call, parse args
4. Otherwise -> field access (the `[...]` will be handled as a subsequent postfix index op in the next loop iteration)

## 4. Error Recovery Strategy

### 4.1 Synchronization Points

The parser defines synchronization points â€” tokens where it's safe to resume parsing after an error:

- **Statement level**: `Semicolon`, `RBrace`, `Let`, `Return`, `If`, `For`, `While`, `Fn`
- **Item level**: `Fn`, `Struct`, `Type`, `Import`, `Annotation(_)`, `Eof`
- **Expression level**: `Semicolon`, `RParen`, `RBracket`, `RBrace`, `Comma`

### 4.2 Recovery Functions

```rust
/// Skip tokens until we reach a statement-level sync point.
fn synchronize_stmt(&mut self)

/// Skip tokens until we reach an item-level sync point.
fn synchronize_item(&mut self)

/// Skip tokens until we reach one of the specified token kinds.
fn synchronize_to(&mut self, stops: &[TokenKind])
```

### 4.3 Recovery Protocol

When a parse function encounters an unexpected token:

1. **Record the error** with span and context (expected vs. found).
2. **Determine recovery scope**:
   - In `parse_stmt`: call `synchronize_stmt()`, return `None` for this statement, continue parsing the block.
   - In `parse_item`: call `synchronize_item()`, return `None` for this item, continue parsing the module.
   - In `parse_expr`: return an error expression or propagate up. Expression-level errors typically bubble up to the statement level.
3. **Continue parsing** from the synchronization point.

### 4.4 Missing Semicolons

Semicolons are required but we provide helpful recovery:
- If we expect `;` but see a token that could start a new statement (`let`, `return`, `if`, `for`, `while`, `}`), record a `MissingSemicolon` error and continue WITHOUT consuming the next token.
- This prevents cascading errors from a single missing semicolon.

### 4.5 Unmatched Delimiters

For `(`, `[`, `{`:
- Track the opening delimiter's span.
- If we reach EOF or an obviously wrong delimiter while looking for the closer, record a `MissingClosingDelimiter` error with both the opening span and current span.
- The opening span helps AI agents locate WHERE the unmatched delimiter was opened.

## 5. Special Parsing Challenges

### 5.1 `@complexity O(n^3)` â€” Free-form Annotation

The `@complexity` annotation takes a complexity expression that is NOT a standard AXIOM expression (it uses `O(...)` notation with `^` for exponentiation). The parser handles this by:

1. After seeing `@complexity`, collect all tokens until the next newline-adjacent statement start or `fn` keyword.
2. Reconstruct the source text from the token spans: `source[first_span.start..last_span.end]`.
3. Store as `Annotation::Complexity(String)`.

Practical implementation: After `@complexity`, consume tokens until we see: `Fn`, another `Annotation(_)`, `Semicolon`, `LBrace`, or `Eof`. Join the source text between those spans.

### 5.2 `@constraint { correctness: "IEEE 754 compliant" };` â€” Trailing Semicolon

Module-level annotations may optionally be followed by a semicolon. The parser eats an optional semicolon after any module-level annotation.

### 5.3 `@target { cpu.simd }` â€” Dot-Separated Identifiers

Inside `@target { ... }`, identifiers can be dot-separated: `cpu.simd`, `gpu.compute`. Parse these as:
1. Consume `Ident`
2. While next is `Dot` followed by `Ident`, consume and append with dot.
3. Commas separate multiple targets.
4. Result: `Annotation::Target(vec!["cpu.simd".to_string()])`

### 5.4 Parameter-Level Annotations

In `matmul_naive.axm`:
```
a: tensor[f32, M, K] @layout(row_major) @align(64),
```

After parsing the type expression `tensor[f32, M, K]`, check if the next token is an `Annotation`. If so, parse annotations and attach them to the `Param` node.

### 5.5 Return Type Annotations

```
) -> tensor[f32, M, N] @layout(row_major) {
```

After parsing the return type, check for annotations before the opening `{` of the function body. These are attached to the function's annotations list (or a separate return_type_annotations field if we add one).

### 5.6 `range(M)` and `range(2, n + 1)` â€” Built-in Calls

These are just regular function calls. `range` is parsed as `Expr::Ident("range")` and the call syntax produces `Expr::Call { func: Expr::Ident("range"), args: [...] }`.

### 5.7 `widen(n)` â€” Conversion Keywords

The tokens `Widen`, `Narrow`, `Truncate` are keyword tokens (not identifiers). In expression position, they behave like function calls. The prefix parser checks for these keywords and parses them as: keyword -> `(` -> expr -> `)`, producing `Expr::Call { func: Expr::Ident("widen"), args: [expr] }`. This treats them uniformly with regular calls for downstream processing.

### 5.8 Disambiguating `<` â€” Comparison vs. Generic

AXIOM has no generics syntax using `<>` â€” tensor types use `[]`. So `<` is always the less-than comparison operator. No ambiguity.

## 6. AST Modifications Required

The existing `ast.rs` needs minor modifications:

1. **Block struct**: Add `annotations` field for strategy blocks inside function bodies:
   ```rust
   pub struct Block {
       pub annotations: Vec<Spanned<Annotation>>,
       pub stmts: Vec<Spanned<Stmt>>,
   }
   ```

2. No other structural changes needed â€” the existing AST types are comprehensive.

## 7. `--emit=ast` Driver Integration

In `axiom-driver/src/main.rs`, the `Some("ast")` branch:

```rust
Some("ast") => {
    let result = axiom_parser::parse(&source);
    if !result.errors.is_empty() {
        for err in &result.errors {
            eprintln!("{:?}", err);
        }
    }
    println!("{:#?}", result.module);
}
```

This uses `Debug` formatting for now. A pretty-printer is a future enhancement.

## 8. Error Types Design

```rust
use miette::{Diagnostic, SourceSpan};
use thiserror::Error;

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Error, Diagnostic)]
pub enum ParseError {
    #[error("expected {expected}, found {found}")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::parse::unexpected_token))]
    UnexpectedToken {
        expected: String,
        found: String,
        #[label("unexpected token here")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },

    #[error("unexpected end of file, expected {expected}")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::parse::unexpected_eof))]
    UnexpectedEof {
        expected: String,
        #[label("file ends here")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },

    #[error("invalid annotation @{name}: {reason}")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::parse::invalid_annotation))]
    InvalidAnnotation {
        name: String,
        reason: String,
        #[label("this annotation")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },

    #[error("invalid type expression: {detail}")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::parse::invalid_type))]
    InvalidTypeExpression {
        detail: String,
        #[label("here")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },

    #[error("missing semicolon")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::parse::missing_semicolon), help("add `;` at the end of this statement"))]
    MissingSemicolon {
        #[label("expected `;` after this")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },

    #[error("missing closing `{delimiter}`")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::parse::missing_delimiter))]
    MissingClosingDelimiter {
        delimiter: char,
        #[label("opened here")]
        open_span: SourceSpan,
        #[label("expected closing `{delimiter}` before here")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },

    #[error("invalid expression: {detail}")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::parse::invalid_expression))]
    InvalidExpression {
        detail: String,
        #[label("here")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },

    #[error("lexer error: {message}")]
    #[diagnostic(code(axiom::lex::error))]
    LexerError {
        message: String,
        #[label("here")]
        span: SourceSpan,
    },
}

pub struct ParseResult {
    pub module: Module,
    pub errors: Vec<ParseError>,
}

impl ParseResult {
    pub fn has_errors(&self) -> bool {
        !self.errors.is_empty()
    }
}
```

Note: `miette::SourceSpan` is a `(offset, length)` tuple. Convert from our `Span { start, end }` with `SourceSpan::new(start.into(), (end - start).into())`.

## 9. Testing Strategy

All tests go in `crates/axiom-parser/src/parser.rs` in a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block.

### Unit Test Categories

1. **Expression precedence tests** (`test_precedence_*`)
2. **Annotation parsing tests** (`test_annotation_*`)
3. **Strategy block tests** (`test_strategy_block_*`)
4. **Type expression tests** (`test_type_expr_*`)
5. **Statement tests** (`test_stmt_*`)
6. **Full module tests** (`test_module_*`)
7. **Error recovery tests** (`test_error_recovery_*`)
8. **Sample file tests** (`test_sample_*`)

### Snapshot Tests

Use `insta` for snapshot testing of AST output from sample files. This catches unintended AST structure changes.

acceptance_tests.name: test_parse_hello
acceptance_tests.description: Parse hello.axm into Module with @module, @intent annotations and a main function
acceptance_tests.expected: Module { name: Some("hello"), annotations: [@intent(...)], items: [Function { name: "main", params: [], return_type: Named("i32"), body: { print(...); return 0; } }] }

acceptance_tests.name: test_parse_fibonacci
acceptance_tests.description: Parse fibonacci.axm â€” two functions, annotations, if/else, for loop, let bindings, binary ops, function calls
acceptance_tests.expected: Module with name 'fibonacci', 2 functions (fib and main), @pure and @complexity annotations on fib, if/else with return, for loop with range(), let bindings, binary ops (+, <=), widen() call

acceptance_tests.name: test_parse_matmul_naive
acceptance_tests.description: Parse matmul_naive.axm â€” annotations with args, tensor types, strategy blocks with ?holes, nested for loops, multi-index expressions
acceptance_tests.expected: Module with name 'matmul', @constraint and @target module annotations, function with tensor params with @layout/@align annotations, @strategy block with ?hole values, tensor type expressions with dimension params, nested for loops, a[i,k] multi-index expressions

acceptance_tests.name: test_precedence_mul_over_add
acceptance_tests.expected: BinaryOp { op: Add, lhs: Ident(a), rhs: BinaryOp { op: Mul, lhs: Ident(b), rhs: Ident(c) } }

acceptance_tests.name: test_precedence_comparison_over_logic
acceptance_tests.expected: BinaryOp { op: And, lhs: BinaryOp { op: Eq, lhs: a, rhs: b }, rhs: BinaryOp { op: NotEq, lhs: c, rhs: d } }

acceptance_tests.name: test_precedence_unary_neg
acceptance_tests.description: Verify unary minus binds tighter than binary operators
acceptance_tests.expected: BinaryOp { op: Add, lhs: UnaryOp { op: Neg, operand: Ident(a) }, rhs: Ident(b) }

acceptance_tests.name: test_precedence_call_and_index
acceptance_tests.description: Verify call and index bind tightest
acceptance_tests.expected: BinaryOp { op: Add, lhs: Index { expr: a, indices: [i] }, rhs: Call { func: f, args: [x] } }

acceptance_tests.name: test_precedence_chained_postfix
acceptance_tests.description: Verify chained field access and method call
acceptance_tests.expected: MethodCall { expr: FieldAccess { expr: FieldAccess { expr: a, field: b }, field: c... wait, no: FieldAccess { expr: a, field: b } then .c(x) is MethodCall { expr: FieldAccess{a,b}, method: c, args: [x] } }

acceptance_tests.name: test_left_associativity
acceptance_tests.expected: BinaryOp { op: Sub, lhs: BinaryOp { op: Sub, lhs: a, rhs: b }, rhs: c }

acceptance_tests.name: test_grouped_expression
acceptance_tests.expected: BinaryOp { op: Mul, lhs: BinaryOp { op: Add, lhs: a, rhs: b }, rhs: c }

acceptance_tests.name: test_annotations_pure
acceptance_tests.expected: Annotation::Pure

acceptance_tests.name: test_annotations_inline
acceptance_tests.expected: Annotation::Inline(InlineHint::Always)

acceptance_tests.name: test_annotations_intent

acceptance_tests.name: test_annotations_constraint_kv

acceptance_tests.name: test_strategy_block_with_holes

acceptance_tests.expected: Annotation::Strategy(StrategyBlock { entries: [(tiling, Map{M: Hole(tile_m), N: Hole(tile_n)}), (order, Hole(loop_order)), (unroll, Hole(unroll_factor))] })

acceptance_tests.name: test_type_expr_named

acceptance_tests.input: if x > 0 { return x; } else { return 0; }
acceptance_tests.expected: Stmt::If { condition: BinaryOp{Gt, x, 0}, then_block: Block{[Return(x)]}, else_block: Some(Block{[Return(0)]}) }

acceptance_tests.name: test_wrapping_operators
acceptance_tests.expected: Correct AST with AddWrap, SubSat, MulWrap operators respecting same-tier precedence (left-assoc)

edge_cases.name: Missing semicolon recovery
edge_cases.input:
let x: i32 = 1
let y: i32 = 2;
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Parser records MissingSemicolon error for first let, then parses second let successfully. Two statements in output, one error.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: When expecting `;` but see `let`, record error, do NOT consume `let`, continue parsing

edge_cases.name: Missing closing brace
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Parser records MissingClosingDelimiter error with span of opening `{`. Returns partial function with body containing the return statement.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: When parsing block and hit EOF, record error, close the block implicitly

edge_cases.name: Multiple errors in one file
edge_cases.input:
fn foo( -> i32 { return 1; }
fn bar() -> { return 2; }
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Two errors collected: missing `)` in foo's params, missing return type in bar. Both functions partially parsed.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: After error in foo params, sync to `{`. After missing type in bar, sync to `{`.

edge_cases.name: Invalid token in expression
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Error on unexpected `@` in expression position. Recovery produces partial expression.

edge_cases.name: Empty function body
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Valid parse â€” empty block. No error (the type system / HIR will catch missing return).

edge_cases.name: Nested unmatched delimiters
edge_cases.input: fn foo() -> i32 { if true { return 1; }
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Missing closing `}` for the function body. The if block closes fine. Error points to the function's `{`.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: Track delimiter nesting depth. On EOF, report outermost unmatched delimiter.

edge_cases.name: Annotation on wrong item
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Parse succeeds syntactically â€” annotation is attached to the let statement context. Semantic validation (in HIR) will reject @pure on a let binding, not the parser.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: Parser does not validate annotation semantics â€” it only parses structure.

edge_cases.name: Strategy block outside function
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Strategy block is parsed as a module-level annotation. Semantic validation happens later.

edge_cases.name: Deeply nested expressions
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Correct AST with proper nesting. No stack overflow for reasonable depth.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: N/A â€” valid syntax. Consider adding a nesting depth limit (e.g., 256) to prevent stack overflow on adversarial input.

edge_cases.name: Lexer error tokens forwarded
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Lexer produces Error token for `$`. Parser wraps it as ParseError::LexerError and continues.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: When encountering TokenKind::Error in any parse position, record LexerError and skip the token.

edge_cases.name: Trailing comma in parameter list
edge_cases.input: fn foo(a: i32, b: i64,) -> i32 { return 0; }
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Valid parse â€” trailing comma is allowed in parameter lists.

edge_cases.name: Empty module
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Valid parse â€” Module with no name, no annotations, no items.

edge_cases.name: Module with only annotations
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Valid parse â€” Module with name 'test' and intent annotation, zero items.

edge_cases.name: Chained comparison operators (no chaining)
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Parses as BinaryOp { Lt, BinaryOp { Lt, a, b }, c } â€” left-associative. No special chaining semantics. Semantic analysis may reject this.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: N/A â€” valid syntax at parse level

edge_cases.name: Strategy block with nested maps and no commas between top-level entries
edge_cases.expected_behavior: All 3 top-level entries parsed. The inner map uses commas. Top-level entries are separated by detecting `IDENT ':'` pattern.
edge_cases.recovery_strategy: Lookahead: if current token is Ident and next is Colon, start a new entry. Otherwise, error.

implementation_notes.token_comparison: Many TokenKind variants carry data (Ident(String), IntLiteral{...}, etc.). For `check`/`eat` operations that only care about the variant discriminant, use `std::mem::discriminant`. For extracting data, use dedicated methods like `eat_ident() -> Option<String>` that pattern-match and extract.
implementation_notes.span_construction: Use `Span::new(start, end)` where start is the span of the first token in the production and end is the span of the last token. For `Spanned<T>`, compute the merged span across all constituent tokens using `Span::merge`.
implementation_notes.miette_integration: Convert `Span { start, end }` to `miette::SourceSpan` via `SourceSpan::from((start as usize, (end - start) as usize))`. The driver must wrap the source text in `miette::NamedSource` for rendering.
implementation_notes.performance: The parser operates on a pre-allocated Vec<Token> with index-based access. No iterator overhead. Token cloning is minimal â€” we clone strings only when building AST nodes. For the benchmark criteria (fibonacci < 10ms, matmul < 20ms), this design is more than sufficient.
implementation_notes.nesting_depth_guard: Add a `depth: u32` counter to the Parser struct. Increment on entering recursive calls (parse_expr, parse_block). If depth exceeds 256, return an error instead of recursing. This prevents stack overflow on adversarial input.
implementation_notes.ast_rs_modification: The Block struct needs an `annotations` field. This is a MINOR change that must be made to `ast.rs` before the parser can handle strategy blocks inside function bodies. The coder agent should make this change as part of the parser implementation.
implementation_notes.handling_annotation_token: The lexer produces `TokenKind::Annotation(String)` where the String is the annotation name WITHOUT the `@` prefix. So `@pure` produces `Annotation("pure")`. The parser dispatches on this string to build the appropriate `Annotation` enum variant.

dependency_graph.axiom-lexer: provides Token, TokenKind, Span, Lexer â€” already complete (M1.1)

axiom-parser.error.rs: new â€” ParseError enum, ParseResult struct
axiom-parser.parser.rs: new â€” Parser struct, all parse_* functions, Pratt expression parser
axiom-parser.lib.rs: modify â€” add module declarations and re-exports

estimated_effort.parser.rs: ~1200 lines â€” largest file, contains all parsing logic
estimated_effort.error.rs: ~120 lines â€” error types and conversion helpers
estimated_effort.lib.rs_changes: ~10 lines â€” module declarations and re-exports
estimated_effort.ast.rs_changes: ~3 lines â€” add annotations field to Block
